skill.name: model-discovery
skill.description: Fetch current AI model names from Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, and Ollama APIs, then classify models into fast, default, and heavy tiers.
skill.summary: Discover current AI model IDs and tier recommendations across major providers.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many credential, network, external command, and filesystem patterns. Manual review confirms legitimate model discovery behavior, but API keys are passed to curl subprocesses and Gemini keys are placed in URLs, so publication should carry a security warning. No evidence found of malicious exfiltration, prompt injection, dynamic code execution, or command injection.

medium_findings.title: Provider API Keys Passed to Curl Subprocesses
medium_findings.description: The fetch script reads Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gemini API keys from the environment and passes them to curl subprocesses. This is legitimate for model discovery, but secrets may be exposed to local process inspection, command diagnostics, or provider network calls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct environment reads feed curl arguments for official provider endpoints. The behavior is intentional, but the local secret exposure risk is real.

medium_findings.title: Gemini API Key Embedded in Request URL
medium_findings.description: The Gemini fetch path places the API key in the query string. Query-string credentials are more likely to appear in logs, traces, proxies, and process listings than header-based credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code and documentation explicitly construct URLs containing the key parameter. This is a known credential handling weakness even when the endpoint is legitimate.

medium_findings.title: Parent Directory Dotenv Loading Can Import Unrelated Secrets
medium_findings.description: The script searches from its own path through parent directories for a .env file and loads the first match. This may import broader workspace secrets that are not needed for model discovery.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dotenv search is clear in the code. Impact depends on workspace layout and what secrets are present, so the risk is contextual.

medium_findings.title: Configurable Ollama Host Enables Arbitrary Model Inventory Requests
medium_findings.description: OLLAMA_HOST controls the target used for Ollama model discovery. This is useful for remote Ollama, but a manipulated environment can direct the script to a non-local HTTP endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The target is environment-controlled and used in a network request. No provider credentials are sent to this endpoint, so the risk is moderate.

medium_findings.title: Cookbook Shell Examples Handle Live API Credentials
medium_findings.description: The cookbook files provide curl and jq examples for Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gemini model APIs. These examples are expected, but they can expose credentials through process arguments when copied directly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly use environment-provided API keys in command lines. They target official APIs, so this is documentation risk rather than malicious execution.

low_findings.title: Local Cache and Registry Files Are Written Automatically
low_findings.description: The scripts write fetched model data to cache/models.json and update known model registry files. This is expected behavior, but users should know the skill modifies local files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write operations are explicit and scoped to skill cache/config paths. This is not malicious, but it is a real filesystem side effect.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Appear to Be False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-crypto alerts point to schema text, model names, tier patterns, or SHA256 digest documentation rather than cryptographic code. No evidence found of weak hash or cipher use for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found model metadata and documentation, not cryptographic implementation. The scanner likely matched words such as digest or model identifiers.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Findings Are Mostly Intended Tooling
low_findings.description: The scripts call curl through subprocess.run with argument lists and fixed provider commands. No shell=True usage or user-controlled shell string construction was found in reviewed code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls use list arguments and bounded provider choices. The remaining concern is credential exposure, not command injection.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Finding Appears to Be a False Positive
low_findings.description: The reported path traversal location is an interactive status print before classification. No evidence found that user-supplied paths are joined or used for file access at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the reported lines shows only output text. File writes use fixed config paths elsewhere in the script.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found in Reviewed Files
low_findings.description: Searches for override language, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, and instructions to skip security analysis found no evidence beyond ordinary Python regex flags.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review did not find prompt-injection text. Confidence is high for reviewed files, but not absolute because all marketplace reviews are bounded by available files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Provider API Keys Passed to Curl Subprocesses
dangerous_patterns.description: The fetch script reads Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gemini API keys from the environment and passes them to curl subprocesses. This is legitimate for model discovery, but secrets may be exposed to local process inspection, command diagnostics, or provider network calls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct environment reads feed curl arguments for official provider endpoints. The behavior is intentional, but the local secret exposure risk is real.

dangerous_patterns.title: Gemini API Key Embedded in Request URL
dangerous_patterns.description: The Gemini fetch path places the API key in the query string. Query-string credentials are more likely to appear in logs, traces, proxies, and process listings than header-based credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code and documentation explicitly construct URLs containing the key parameter. This is a known credential handling weakness even when the endpoint is legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Configurable Ollama Host Enables Arbitrary Model Inventory Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: OLLAMA_HOST controls the target used for Ollama model discovery. This is useful for remote Ollama, but a manipulated environment can direct the script to a non-local HTTP endpoint.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The target is environment-controlled and used in a network request. No provider credentials are sent to this endpoint, so the risk is moderate.

content.value_statement: Model IDs change often, which can break API calls and routing rules. This skill fetches provider model lists and maps them into practical tiers.
actual_capabilities: Fetches model lists from Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, and local Ollama endpoints.
Caches provider responses in a local models cache with a freshness check.
Maps known models into fast, default, and heavy capability tiers.
Detects provider models that are not yet in the known model registry.
Supports automatic tier inference from model name patterns.
Provides cookbook commands for direct provider API checks.
limitations: Requires valid provider API keys for Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gemini discovery.
Runs curl subprocesses and makes outbound network requests to provider APIs.
Model tier mappings can become stale when providers release new models.
Ollama results depend on the local or configured Ollama server.

use_cases.title: Refresh model references before deployment
use_cases.description: Verify current provider model IDs before updating production configuration or release notes.

use_cases.title: Choose model tiers for agent routing
use_cases.description: Use fast, default, and heavy mappings when selecting models for different task complexity levels.

use_cases.title: Track new provider model releases
use_cases.description: Compare fetched model lists with the known registry and flag models that need classification.

prompt_templates.title: Check Cached Models
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check before using a model name.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use model-discovery to check whether the cached model list is fresh. Tell me which model IDs are available for my provider.

prompt_templates.title: Refresh One Provider
prompt_templates.scenario: Update a single provider model list.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use model-discovery to refresh OpenAI models only. Summarize the current fast, default, and heavy options.

prompt_templates.title: Find New Models
prompt_templates.scenario: Review provider changes after a refresh.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use model-discovery to detect new models. List any unclassified models and suggest a tier for each one.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Routing Choices
prompt_templates.prompt: Use model-discovery to compare Claude, Codex, Gemini, and Ollama tier mappings. Recommend routing defaults for latency-sensitive, balanced, and high-reasoning tasks.

output_examples.input: Refresh current OpenAI model names and recommend tiers.
output_examples.output: The skill reports a fast option, a balanced default option, and a heavy reasoning option. It notes any missing API key or provider error.

output_examples.input: Check whether any provider models are new.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a provider-by-provider summary with counts, inferred tiers, and models that need manual classification.

output_examples.input: Pick a model for a quick code review in Claude Code and Codex.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends fast or default tier models and explains when to switch to a heavy tier for deeper reasoning.

best_practices: Run cache-only checks first when fresh data is not required.
Use least-privilege API keys and avoid running the skill in shared shells.
Review new model classifications before committing updated tier mappings.
anti_patterns: Do not paste provider API keys directly into prompts or command examples.
Do not assume cached model lists are current for production releases.
Do not point OLLAMA_HOST at untrusted endpoints without reviewing the target.

faq.question: Does this skill call external APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. It calls Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, and configured Ollama endpoints when refreshing model lists.

faq.question: Does it require API keys?
faq.answer: Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gemini discovery require provider API keys. Ollama discovery does not require a key by default.

faq.question: Can it update files in my workspace?
faq.answer: Yes. It can write cache data and update the known model registry during classification workflows.

faq.question: Can it choose the best model automatically?
faq.answer: It suggests fast, default, and heavy tiers. You should review final choices for cost, latency, and quality needs.

faq.question: Is it useful for Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes mappings for Claude Code and Codex model families when those mappings are present in the config.

faq.question: What should I verify before publishing results?
faq.answer: Verify API errors, cache age, new model classifications, and whether any provider credentials were exposed in logs.
